senses.gloss: to cut off, to chop off
equal of a person or thing
Indicates nearness in time or recentness, recently, just now

examples.english: he has arrived; he had arrived
If you ask him for money, he may give you.

examples.text: قَدْ يَعْلَمُ اللَّهُ الْمُعَوِّقِينَ مِنْكُمْ وَٱلْقَائِلِينَ لِإِخْوَانِهِمْ هَلُمَّ إِلَيْنَا وَلَا يَأْتُونَ الْبَأْسَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا
examples.english: God does know the hinderers among you and the ones telling their brethren, "Over here!" These fight in wars only rarely.

etymology.text:
Inherited from Proto-Semitic, probably of onomatopoeic origin. See قَتَّ (qatta).
Cognate to Hebrew קָדַד (qāḏaḏ), Aramaic קָדַד (qāḏaḏ, “to cut out”), Classical Syriac ܩܰܕ (qadd, “to tear or cut away”), Ge'ez ቀደ (ḳäddä, “to cut open, to tear apart”), Mehri ḳdd (“to shave off, to cut off, to scalp”).

From the root ق د د (q d d) in the sense of being a thing separated or cut apart, a thing distinct with its own measure and shape, a defined piece; extended semantically to a secure definite thing, a certainty, hence the past tense implying a thing has already been set or definitive.
The non-past tense stems from the idea of something intended, likely expected, planned to be, measured to occur, or cut out to happen.

Borrowed from English cod, as the range of the fish does not overlap with typical Arabic-speaking areas; compare Hebrew קוד (kod, “cod”). Compare also its other names مُورَة (mūra, “cod”) borrowed from French morue, and غادُس (ḡādus, “cod”) borrowed from Latin gadus (“generic term for fish; cod”). But the word قُدّ as a type of fish appears in two early Arabic-Arabic dictionaries, the Qamus of al-Fīrūzābādī (1329–1414) and the 'Ubaab of al-Saghānī (1181-1252), suggesting that a borrowing from English is most improbable. Perhaps from Greek γάδος?

(Mughnee, K. *)       -A2-  As a particle, it is used peculiarly with a verb, (Mughnee, K,) [i. e.] as such it is not preposed to anything except a verb, (S, O,) either a pret. or an aor., (TA,) from which it is not separated unless by an oath, (Mughnee,) such as is perfectly inflected, enunciative, (Mughnee, K,) not an imperative, (TA,) affirmative, and free from anything that would render it mejzoom or man- soob, and from what is termed
and its variants]: and it has six meanings. (Mughnee, K.) ―         -b2-  (1) It denotes expectation: (M, Mughnee, K:) and when it is with an aor., this is evident; (Mughnee;) one says
It is expected that the absent will come:
(TA:) and most affirm that it is thus used with a pret.: (Mughnee:) accord. to some, (M,) it is used in reply to the saying
[i. e. “ He has not yet done ” such a thing, which implies expectation that he would do it]; (S, M, O;) the reply being,
the thing]: (M:) and Kh asserts that it is used in reply to persons expecting information; (S, M, * O, Mughnee;) [for to such] you say,
Already such a one has died
]; but if one inform him who does not expect it, he does not say thus, but he says [merely]
his horse] to him who expects his mounting: some, however, disallow that

is used to denote expectation with the pret. because the pret. denotes what is already past; and hence it appears that those who affirm it to be so used mean that the pret. denotes what was expected before the information: (Mughnee: [in which it is added, with some other observations, that, in the opinion of its author, it does not denote expectation even with the aor.; because the saying

:]) MF says, What we have been orally taught by the sheykhs in ElAndalus is this, that it is a particle denoting the affirmation of truth, or certainty, when it occurs before a pret., and a particle denoting expectation when it occurs before a future. (TA.) ―         -b3-  (2) It denotes the nearness of the past to the present: (O, Mughnee, K:) so in the saying
a meaning often intended by saying merely,
may mean the near past and the remote past; (Mughnee;) and so in the saying of the muëdhdhin,
The time of the rising to prayer has just come,
]: (O:) [and, when thus used, it is often immediately preceded by the pret. or aor. of the verb
] and accord. to the Basrees, except Akh, it must be either expressed or understood immediately before a pret. used as a denotative of state; as in [the saying in the Kur ii. 247,]

(Mughnee.)
